Two mechanisms of nucleophilic substitution
Both give the same overall reaction, but the mechanisms are unique

Or let some cats explain the difference

2 Properties of a good leaving group (L.G.)
Electronegative (to make a highly polarized and weak C-X bond)
Stable after they leave with electrons (and usually negative charge) weaker base = better leaving group
Leaving Groups

Leaving Groups: Halides
Good leaving groups
Substrate | Leaving group
R-Cl
R-Br
R-I
R-OSO2R’
R-+OH2
R-+OHR’
R-+NR’3 | Cl- (chloride)
Br- (bromide)
I- (iodide)
R’SO3- (sulfonate)
H2O (water)
R’OH (alcohol)
NR’3 (amine)

Hydroxide is a dreadful leaving group:
Can be converted into a good leaving group with the addition of acid:

Epoxides (oxirane rings) are cyclic ethers which have very strained rings
Epoxides can react with nucleophiles such as alkoxides and amines

Nucleophile strength = “ nucleophilicity ”:
how fast a nucleophile reacts in a substitution reaction    (strong / weak)
In general, a strong base is also a strong nucleophile since both bases and nucleophiles seek positive charges
- basicity relates to relative thermodynamic stability between the base and its conjugate acid
- nucleophilicity relates to a kinetic process (reaction rate)
Nucleophile strength

3 Trends in Nucleophilicity
A species with a negative charge is a stronger nucleophile than a similar neutral species
More electronegative elements are less nucleophilic
Larger atoms are more nucleophilic (better orbital overlap/similar energy)

Other Effects on Nucleophile Strength
Steric effects: bulky nucleophiles are weaker
2)  Solvent effects: nucleophiles are strongest in polar, aprotic solvents Polar, protic solvents Polar, aprotic solvents
Solvent effects: nucleophiles are strongest in polar, aprotic solvents

Table of common nucleophile structures, names, and relative strength and basicity
Strong nucleophiles | Weak(ish) bases: 
I- (iodide), 
N3- (azide), 
-CN (cyanide, makes nitriles), 
:PR3 (phosphine, makes phosphonium), 
RS-/HS- (thiolate/sulfide)

Strong bases: 
RO-/HO- (alkoxide/hydroxide), 
RC≡C- (acetylide), 
NH2- (amide, makes amines),
Moderate  nucleophiles | Br-/Cl- (bromide/chloride), 
RNH2/NH3 (amine/ammonia), 
RSH (thiol), 
RCOO- (carboxylate)
Weak nucleophiles | F- (fluoride), 
RCOOH (carboxylic acid), 
ROH/H2O (alcohol/water)

Special note regarding substitution reactions with amines and thiols:
Problem with amines: product mixture and protonation
Solution: substitute with azide , then reduce

Special note regarding substitution reactions with amines and thiols:
Can easily deprotonate thiols to make thiolate anion
Issue with making thiols using NaSH

Special note regarding iodide S N 2 catalysis
In general, a good leaving group makes a poor nucleophile, and a poor nucleophile makes a good leaving group.
Iodide is both a -good leaving group
-strong nucleophile
